requirements: The core contributions of the paper 'Niyama: Breaking the Silos of LLM Inference Serving' have been reproduced.

sub_tasks.requirements: The infrastructure, models, and datasets used in the paper have been acquired and prepared.
Code has been implemented to load the Llama3-8B model for inference on a single A100 GPU with 80GB memory.

verification.spec: Run the inference engine initialization with model='meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B' on a single A100 80GB GPU. Verify the model loads successfully, fits in memory, and can complete a sample generation. Check that tensor_parallel_size=1 is used.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to load the Qwen-7B model for inference across two A100 GPUs (tensor parallelism TP=2).

verification.spec: Run inference engine initialization with model='Qwen/Qwen-7B' with tensor_parallel_size=2 across two A100 GPUs. Verify the model loads and a sample inference completes successfully.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to download and prepare the ShareGPT dataset, preserving prompt and decode token counts (p50: 1730/415, p90: 5696/834).

verification.spec: Run the ShareGPT data preparation script. Compute p50 and p90 of prompt token lengths and decode token lengths over the loaded dataset. Verify p50 prompt ~1730, p90 prompt ~5696, p50 decode ~415, p90 decode ~834.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to download and prepare the Azure Conv trace dataset, preserving prompt and decode token counts (p50: 928/41, p90: 3830/342).

verification.spec: Run the Azure Conv trace preparation script. Compute p50 and p90 of prompt and decode token lengths. Verify p50 prompt ~928, p90 prompt ~3830, p50 decode ~41, p90 decode ~342.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to download and prepare the Azure Code trace dataset, preserving prompt and decode token counts (p50: 1930/8, p90: 6251/43).

verification.spec: Run the Azure Code trace preparation script. Compute p50 and p90 of prompt and decode token lengths. Verify p50 prompt ~1930, p90 prompt ~6251, p50 decode ~8, p90 decode ~43.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to generate request arrival times using a Poisson distribution at a configurable QPS.

verification.spec: Call the arrival-time generator with a fixed QPS (e.g., 4.0) and a fixed seed for n=10000 requests. Compute inter-arrival times. Verify they are approximately exponential with mean 1/QPS (e.g., via a chi-square or KS test against Exp(rate=QPS), p>0.01) and that mean inter-arrival is within 5% of 1/QPS.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to divide each evaluation dataset into three equal partitions, each assigned to one of the three QoS buckets (Q1, Q2, Q3) at a 33.33% / 33.33% / 33.33% ratio as per Table 2.

verification.spec: Run the workload partitioning script on each dataset. Verify that requests are partitioned into 3 disjoint groups, each containing 33.33% (+/- 1 request) of the total, and that each group is tagged with QoS bucket Q1, Q2, or Q3 respectively per Table 2.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to set up the Sarathi-Serve / vLLM base inference framework, including PagedAttention and tensor parallelism support.

verification.spec: Import the Sarathi-Serve / vLLM base engine. Initialize it with a small model and run a single forward pass. Verify that PagedAttention KV-cache blocks are allocated (inspect block_manager) and that tensor_parallel_size>1 path is available in the configuration API.

sub_tasks.requirements: QoS classes, deadlines, and SLO definitions have been implemented as per Section 3.2.
Code has been implemented so that an inference request can be tagged with one of two QoS classes (interactive or non-interactive) at submission via an extended vLLM API.

verification.spec: Submit two requests via the extended vLLM API: one tagged qos_class='interactive' and one tagged qos_class='non-interactive'. Verify both are accepted, and that querying the request's metadata returns the correct qos_class. Submitting an invalid qos_class string should raise a validation error.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that an interactive request carries a TTFT SLO and a TBT SLO, while a non-interactive request carries only a TTLT SLO.

verification.spec: Submit an interactive request with TTFT and TBT SLOs; verify both are stored on the request object and a TTLT field is absent or None. Submit a non-interactive request with a TTLT SLO; verify TTLT is stored and TTFT/TBT are absent/None. Submitting an interactive request without TTFT or TBT should raise an error; submitting a non-interactive without TTLT should raise.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to assign QoS bucket Q1 with TTFT=6s and TBT=50ms to one third of requests in each dataset (interactive class).

verification.spec: Inspect the workload configuration. Verify the Q1 bucket is configured as interactive with TTFT_SLO=6.0 seconds and TBT_SLO=0.050 seconds (50 ms), and that exactly one third of dataset requests are assigned to Q1.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to assign QoS bucket Q2 with TTLT=600s to one third of requests (non-interactive class).

verification.spec: Inspect the workload configuration. Verify the Q2 bucket is configured as non-interactive with TTLT_SLO=600 seconds, and that exactly one third of dataset requests are assigned to Q2.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to assign QoS bucket Q3 with TTLT=1800s to one third of requests (non-interactive class).

verification.spec: Inspect the workload configuration. Verify the Q3 bucket is configured as non-interactive with TTLT_SLO=1800 seconds, and that exactly one third of dataset requests are assigned to Q3.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to compute the first-token deadline for interactive requests as arrival_time + TTFT_SLO (eq 1).

verification.spec: Construct an interactive request with arrival_time=100.0 and TTFT_SLO=6.0. Call the deadline-computation function. Verify the returned first-token deadline equals 106.0 (arrival_time + TTFT_SLO) per eq 1.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to compute the per-token deadline for interactive requests as first_token_deadline + n*TBT_SLO for position n (eq 2).

verification.spec: Construct an interactive request with first_token_deadline=106.0 and TBT_SLO=0.05. For token positions n=1,2,5,10, call the per-token deadline computation. Verify the returned deadlines equal first_token_deadline + n*TBT_SLO (e.g., 106.05, 106.10, 106.25, 106.50) per eq 2.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to compute the completion deadline for non-interactive requests as arrival_time + TTLT_SLO (eq 3).

verification.spec: Construct a non-interactive request with arrival_time=200.0 and TTLT_SLO=600.0. Call the deadline-computation function for non-interactive class. Verify the completion deadline equals 800.0 (arrival_time + TTLT_SLO) per eq 3.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so each request also carries an application-provided priority field (e.g., free vs paid / important vs low-priority) used for relegation decisions.

verification.spec: Submit requests with priority='high' (paid) and priority='low' (free). Verify the priority attribute is stored on each request and is accessible by the scheduler. Verify the relegation policy uses this field by triggering overload and confirming low-priority requests are relegated first.

sub_tasks.requirements: The Niyama scheduler architecture (Section 3.1) has been implemented.
Code has been implemented so the scheduler maintains three distinct request queues: a prefill queue, a decode queue, and a relegated queue.

verification.spec: Import the Niyama scheduler class and inspect its state. Verify it exposes three distinct, separately-addressable queue data structures named (or equivalent to) prefill_queue, decode_queue, and relegated_queue. Confirm they are independent containers, not aliases.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that new requests entering the system are placed in the prefill queue.

verification.spec: Submit a new request to the scheduler. Inspect queue states before running any scheduling iteration. Verify the new request appears in the prefill_queue and not in decode_queue or relegated_queue.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so each iteration builds a mixed batch consisting of all in-flight decode requests plus a single chunked prefill from a selected prefill request.

verification.spec: Populate the decode queue with 3 in-flight requests and the prefill queue with 2 pending requests. Run a single scheduling iteration and inspect the resulting batch. Verify the batch contains decode tokens from all 3 decode-queue requests plus exactly one prefill chunk from a single selected prefill-queue request (not multiple prefill requests in one batch).

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the prefill selector that picks the next prefill request from the prefill queue using the hybrid prioritization policy.

verification.spec: Import the prefill_selector function/class. Construct a synthetic prefill queue with 3+ requests having known TTFT deadlines and remaining-prefill estimates. Verify the selector returns the request maximizing the hybrid priority (eq 4) given a fixed alpha. Test edge cases: empty queue returns None; tie-break is deterministic.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for a violation-checker module that, given the selected prefill request, checks whether its TTFT/TTLT deadline has already been (or will be) violated this iteration.

verification.spec: Import violation_checker. Construct request objects with (i) deadline already in the past, (ii) deadline that will be missed given an estimated iteration latency, and (iii) safely meetable deadline. Verify checker returns violation=True for (i) and (ii), False for (iii).

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that requests flagged by the violation checker are moved to the relegated queue and a different prefill request is selected.

verification.spec: Simulate a scheduler step where the top-priority prefill request is flagged by the violation checker. Verify (a) that request is appended to the relegated_queue, (b) it is removed from the prefill_queue, and (c) the scheduler then selects the next-highest-priority prefill request.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that, after a request finishes its prefill phase, it is moved from the prefill queue to the decode queue.

verification.spec: Construct a mock request, run it through the scheduler until its prefill phase completes, and assert it is removed from the prefill queue and inserted into the decode queue in the next scheduling iteration.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that relegated requests are opportunistically scheduled during periods of low system load and never permanently rejected.

verification.spec: Simulate a high-load phase that pushes a request into the relegated queue, then drop the load below capacity; assert the relegated request is eventually scheduled and completes, and that no code path drops/rejects relegated requests permanently.

sub_tasks.requirements: The dynamic chunking mechanism (Section 3.3) has been implemented.
Code has been implemented so each batch processes a single chunk of prefill tokens fused with all decode tokens, following the chunked-prefill design of Sarathi-Serve.

verification.spec: Construct a state with N decode requests and one prefill request; call the batch-construction routine and assert the resulting batch contains exactly one prefill chunk fused with all N decode tokens (Sarathi-style chunked prefill).

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to compute per-decode-request slack as (next-token deadline from eq 2) minus current time.

verification.spec: Import the slack-computation utility. For a decode request with known next-token deadline T_d and current time t, verify it returns T_d - t. Test with deadline in the future (positive slack), deadline already passed (negative slack), and equal (zero).

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to collect performance profiles from Vidur and to train the chunk-size latency predictor offline.

verification.spec: Verify the repository contains a script that (1) invokes/loads Vidur profiles and (2) trains the random-forest chunk-size latency predictor offline, saving a serialized model artifact. Run the training script end-to-end on a small profile sample and confirm it produces the model file.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that when a strict-TBT interactive decode is active, the chunk size is reduced to satisfy that TBT target (e.g. 256 tokens for a 50ms TBT class).

verification.spec: Construct a decode batch containing at least one interactive request with TBT=50ms; call the dynamic-chunk selector and assert that the chosen chunk size is bounded such that predicted latency <= 50ms (e.g., ~256 tokens for Llama3-8B on A100).

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that when no decode requires a strict TBT, dynamic chunking enlarges the chunk size to maximize throughput while still respecting TTLT deadlines.

verification.spec: Construct a decode batch containing only non-interactive (TTLT-only) requests with large slack; call the chunk-size selector and assert the chosen chunk size is strictly larger than the size chosen when a strict-TBT interactive decode is present, while still keeping predicted finish time within TTLT deadlines.

sub_tasks.requirements: The hybrid prioritization policy (Section 3.4) has been implemented.

Code has been implemented so that interactive request priority is computed as a linear combination of the TTFT deadline (EDF term) and the estimated remaining prefill processing time (SRPF term), with weighting parameter alpha (eq 4).

verification.spec: Import the interactive-priority function. For known TTFT deadline D and estimated remaining-prefill time R, verify the returned priority equals alpha * (EDF term using D) + (1-alpha) * (SRPF term using R) [or the paper's exact equivalent linear combination]. Test alpha=0 reduces to pure SRPF ordering, alpha=1 reduces to pure EDF ordering across a small set of synthetic requests.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that non-interactive request priority is computed as a linear combination of the TTLT deadline and (remaining prefill + estimated remaining decode) processing time (eq 5).

verification.spec: Import the priority computation function for non-interactive requests. Construct mock requests with varying TTLT deadlines, remaining prefill token counts, and estimated remaining decode token counts. Verify the computed priority equals a linear combination alpha*(TTLT_deadline - current_time) + (1-alpha)*(prefill_remaining_time + decode_remaining_time) per eq 5. Test that increasing TTLT_deadline increases priority value (later deadline => lower urgency) and increasing remaining work changes priority monotonically. Also verify decode_remaining is estimated from historical mean + 2 standard deviations.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to estimate Decode_rem for a non-interactive request using a per-application running history of decode lengths, over-approximated by two standard deviations above the mean.

verification.spec: Feed a per-application history of decode lengths (e.g., samples with known mean mu and std sigma) into the Decode_rem estimator and assert the returned estimate equals mu + 2*sigma (in tokens or time units) within a small tolerance.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that the hybrid prioritization parameter alpha is a configurable deployment parameter.

verification.spec: Inspect the scheduler config / CLI / API and verify that alpha (hybrid prioritization interpolation weight between EDF and SRPF) is exposed as a user-settable parameter. Instantiate the scheduler with two different alpha values and assert internal priority computation reflects the change.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that the prefill selector orders the prefill queue by the hybrid priority via a priority queue and picks the highest priority eligible request.

verification.spec: Insert several prefill requests with known deadlines and remaining prefill times into the prefill selector. Compute expected hybrid priorities manually and assert the selector pops them in the correct priority order; verify the underlying data structure is a priority queue (heap).

sub_tasks.requirements: The eager relegation policy (Section 3.4) has been implemented.

Code has been implemented so that a request which has already violated its TTFT or TTLT deadline, or which the violation checker predicts will violate it in the current iteration, is moved to the relegated queue.

verification.spec: Instantiate the Niyama scheduler. Submit a request whose TTFT deadline has already elapsed (or a non-interactive request whose TTLT is past). Run one scheduling iteration and verify the request is moved from the prefill queue to the relegated queue. Then submit a request which the violation checker predicts will violate its deadline if scheduled now (e.g., by mocking the latency predictor to return a value exceeding remaining slack) and confirm it is also relegated.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that, when relegation is required, low-priority requests (e.g., free tier) are selected for relegation before any high-priority ones.

verification.spec: Submit a mixed set of requests tagged with high-priority and low-priority flags (paid vs free tier) all of which are eligible for relegation. Trigger an overload condition. Verify that all low-priority requests are relegated first, and high-priority requests are only relegated once no low-priority requests remain to be relegated.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that high-priority requests are only relegated when no low-priority candidates remain and they have already violated their deadline.

verification.spec: Set up two scenarios: (a) a high-priority request has violated its deadline AND a low-priority candidate exists -> assert the low-priority one is relegated, not the high-priority; (b) same but no low-priority candidate -> assert the violating high-priority request is relegated.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that relegated requests are admitted back for service only when there is spare capacity (no eligible queued non-relegated requests).

verification.spec: Place one request in the relegated queue and one in the prefill queue; assert the prefill request is scheduled first. Then empty the prefill/decode queues and assert the relegated request is admitted for scheduling.

sub_tasks.requirements: The selective preemption policy (Section 3.4) has been implemented.
Code has been implemented so that an in-flight prefill request may be preempted only if its priority is lower than that of a newly admissible request.

verification.spec: Set up an in-flight prefill request with computed priority P1 and an incoming request with priority P2. Assert that preemption occurs iff P2 > P1, and never when P2 <= P1.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that requests in the decode queue are never preempted.

verification.spec: Place several requests into the decode queue and submit a new high-priority interactive request to the prefill queue. Run scheduling iterations. Verify via scheduler state inspection / logs that no decode-queue request is ever preempted (their KV cache and decode progression continue uninterrupted) regardless of incoming high-priority prefills.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that a prefill request is only preempted if pausing it for one iteration does not by itself cause its deadline to be violated.

verification.spec: Construct a prefill request whose deadline is tight enough that skipping one iteration would cause violation; assert the selective-preemption logic refuses to preempt it even when a higher-priority request arrives. Then loosen the slack and assert preemption is allowed.

sub_tasks.requirements: All baseline schedulers used in evaluation have been implemented.
Code has been implemented for the Sarathi-Silo (SOTA) baseline, where each QoS bucket runs on its own independent GPU replica using the Sarathi-Serve scheduler.

verification.spec: Launch the Sarathi-Silo baseline configuration: assert that three independent scheduler instances (one per QoS bucket) are spawned, each using vanilla Sarathi-Serve with the per-bucket chunk size (256 for strict TBT, 2K for others), and that requests are routed only to the replica matching their QoS bucket.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that in the Sarathi-Silo baseline the strict TBT=50ms bucket uses a chunk size of 256, and the two non-interactive buckets use a chunk size of 2K.

verification.spec: Inspect the Sarathi-Silo baseline configuration file or constants. Verify the replica assigned to bucket Q1 (TBT=50ms) is configured with chunk_size=256, and the replicas assigned to buckets Q2 and Q3 (non-interactive) are configured with chunk_size=2048.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the Sarathi-FCFS baseline, co-scheduling all QoS buckets on a single shared cluster using Sarathi-Serve with FCFS prioritization.

verification.spec: Launch the Sarathi-FCFS shared-cluster baseline; submit a mix of QoS-tagged requests in a known order and assert prefill scheduling order matches arrival order regardless of QoS, with chunk size 256.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the Sarathi-EDF baseline, co-scheduling all QoS buckets on a single shared cluster with Earliest Deadline First prefill prioritization.

verification.spec: Launch the Sarathi-EDF shared-cluster baseline; submit requests with known deadlines and assert prefill chunks are dispatched in increasing deadline order, using chunk size 256.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the Sarathi-SRPF baseline that prioritizes jobs with the lowest number of remaining (pending) prefill tokens.

verification.spec: Instantiate the Sarathi-SRPF scheduler; submit prefill requests with varying remaining prefill token counts and assert the next scheduled request is always the one with the smallest remaining prefill tokens (preempting longer ones if necessary).

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for the Sarathi-SJF baseline (used in Figure 2) that prioritizes jobs with the shortest expected total execution time.

verification.spec: Instantiate the Sarathi-SJF scheduler used in Figure 2; submit requests with known expected total execution times and assert scheduling order is ascending by expected execution time.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that all shared-cluster baselines (FCFS, EDF, SRPF, SJF) use a fixed chunk size of 256 to meet the strictest TBT target.

verification.spec: Inspect the configuration / launch scripts for Sarathi-FCFS, Sarathi-EDF, Sarathi-SRPF, and Sarathi-SJF shared-cluster baselines and assert chunk_size is set to 256 in all four.

sub_tasks.requirements: Latency and SLO measurement infrastructure has been implemented.
Code has been implemented to compute per-request TTFT (time from arrival to first generated token).

verification.spec: Run a single request through the system with mocked arrival_time and first-token timestamp; assert the reported TTFT == first_token_time - arrival_time within microsecond precision.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to compute per-request TBT (time between consecutive decode tokens).

verification.spec: Run a request that generates N decode tokens with recorded per-token timestamps; assert the reported TBT series equals the differences between consecutive decode-token timestamps.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to compute per-request TTLT (time from arrival to last generated token).

verification.spec: Run a request with known arrival_time and last-token timestamp; assert the reported TTLT == last_token_time - arrival_time within microsecond precision.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to compute median (p50), p95, and p99 latency statistics per QoS bucket.

verification.spec: Feed a synthetic list of completed requests with known latencies tagged by QoS bucket into the metrics aggregator; assert that returned p50, p95, p99 per bucket match values computed independently via numpy.percentile.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to compute the percentage of requests that violated their SLO (TTFT, TBT, and/or TTLT), overall and per QoS bucket.

verification.spec: Import the SLO violation computation module and verify it accepts a list of completed request records (each with QoS class, TTFT, TBT samples, TTLT, and corresponding deadlines) and returns (a) overall violation percentage and (b) a per-QoS-bucket dict of violation percentages. Feed a small synthetic batch with known violations and assert the returned percentages match the hand-computed values within 0.1%.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to classify requests as 'long' if their prompt length is at or above the 90th percentile of the dataset and as 'short' otherwise, and to report violation rates separately for the two groups.

verification.spec: Call the request classification helper on a synthetic dataset of 100 requests with known prompt lengths; verify that requests with prompt length >= the 90th percentile are tagged 'long' and others 'short'. Then run the violation reporter and confirm it returns separate violation percentages for 'long' and 'short' groups consistent with hand calculations.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to compute system goodput as the number of completed requests per second whose p99 latency targets are met, allowing up to 1% violations.

verification.spec: Invoke the goodput computation routine on a synthetic trace of completed requests with known per-request p99 latencies and deadlines. Verify (a) it computes goodput as completed-requests-meeting-SLO/elapsed-time, and (b) it returns the maximum sustainable QPS such that fewer than 1% of requests violate their p99 SLO. Compare against a hand-computed expected value.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to compute the minimum number of A100 GPUs required to sustain a target aggregate QPS (50) across QoS buckets while violating SLOs for at most 1% of requests.

verification.spec: Run the capacity-planning utility on a stub workload at 50 QPS across three QoS buckets. Mock per-replica goodput values and verify the utility returns ceil(50 / per_replica_goodput) GPUs subject to the <=1% violation constraint. Verify it iterates correctly over replicas and produces the smallest GPU count that meets the constraint.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to log rolling p99 latency over 60-second windows separately for each QoS bucket over a multi-hour run.

verification.spec: Feed the rolling-latency logger a synthetic stream of timestamped request completion events spanning >2 hours and three QoS buckets. Confirm the logger emits a per-bucket time series of p99 latency computed over a sliding 60s window. Validate the output sample at a chosen timestamp matches a hand-computed p99 over the requests in that window.

sub_tasks.requirements: Experiments and numerical results for Section 4.1 (capacity at uniform load) have been reproduced.

An experiment has been executed measuring the number of A100 GPUs needed by Niyama and Sarathi-Silo (SOTA) to serve 50 QPS split evenly across three QoS buckets on Azure Code, Azure Conv, and ShareGPT (Figure 7a).

verification.spec: Run the capacity-evaluation experiment script for Niyama and Sarathi-Silo (SOTA) on each of Azure Code, Azure Conv, and ShareGPT datasets at 50 QPS split equally across the three QoS buckets defined in Table 2. For each (system, dataset) combination, output the minimum number of A100 GPUs required to keep SLO violations <=1%. Confirm all six numbers are produced and that Niyama's GPU count is between 12% and 32% lower than SOTA per Figure 7a (e.g., Azure Code 42 vs 62; Azure Conv 36 vs 41; ShareGPT 66 vs 76).

sub_tasks.requirements: Niyama needs approximately 62 A100 GPUs to serve 50 QPS on the Azure Code dataset, compared with 76 for the SOTA siloed baseline.

verification.spec: Run Niyama on a shared A100-80GB cluster serving 50 QPS total from the Azure Code dataset (split equally across 3 QoS tiers per Table 2). Find the minimum number of replicas needed such that <=1% of requests violate their QoS targets. Report total A100 GPUs used. Expect ~62.

sub_tasks.requirements: Niyama needs approximately 36 A100 GPUs to serve 50 QPS on the Azure Conv dataset, compared with 42 for the SOTA siloed baseline.

verification.spec: Run Niyama on a shared A100-80GB cluster serving 50 QPS total from the Azure Conv dataset (split equally across 3 QoS tiers per Table 2). Find the minimum GPU count meeting <=1% QoS violation; report A100 count. Expect ~36.

sub_tasks.requirements: Niyama needs approximately 66 A100 GPUs to serve 50 QPS on the ShareGPT dataset, compared with 41 for the SOTA siloed baseline (per Figure 7a labels).

verification.spec: Run Niyama on a shared A100-80GB cluster serving 50 QPS total from ShareGPT (split equally across 3 QoS tiers per Table 2). Find the minimum GPU count meeting <=1% QoS violation; report A100 count. Expect ~66.

sub_tasks.requirements: Niyama yields a 12-32% reduction in GPU requirement vs Sarathi-Silo across the three workloads, with Azure Conv specifically showing roughly 32% reduction.

verification.spec: Run both Niyama and Sarathi-Silo on Azure Conv at 50 QPS with 3-tier QoS split. Compute (Sarathi_GPUs - Niyama_GPUs) / Sarathi_GPUs. Expect ~0.32 (32% reduction).

sub_tasks.requirements: Per-replica goodput on Azure Code is approximately 3.65 QPS for Niyama, 2.75 for Sarathi-EDF, and 1.5 for Sarathi-FCFS (Figure 7b).

verification.spec: On a single A100-80GB replica serving Llama3-8B with the Azure-Code workload (3-tier QoS split) over a 4-hour Poisson trace, sweep QPS and measure the maximum sustained QPS (goodput) such that <=1% requests miss their deadline (p99). Report Niyama goodput; expect ~3.65 QPS.

sub_tasks.requirements: Per-replica goodput on Azure Conv is approximately 4.25 QPS for Niyama, 3.55 for Sarathi-EDF, and 2.75 for Sarathi-FCFS (Figure 7b).

verification.spec: On a single A100-80GB replica serving Llama3-8B with the Azure-Conv workload (3-tier QoS split) over a Poisson trace, sweep QPS to find Niyama's max sustained QPS where <=1% miss deadline. Expect ~4.25 QPS.

sub_tasks.requirements: Niyama achieves 1.5x to 2.4x higher goodput than Sarathi-FCFS and 20-40% higher goodput than Sarathi-EDF over a 4-hour Azure-Code run.

verification.spec: Measure per-replica goodput on a 4-hour Azure-Code trace for Niyama, Sarathi-FCFS, and Sarathi-EDF on A100-80GB with Llama3-8B. Compute ratios Niyama/FCFS and Niyama/EDF. Expect Niyama/FCFS in [1.5, 2.4] and Niyama/EDF in [1.20, 1.40].

sub_tasks.requirements: Experiments and numerical results for Section 4.2 (latency and SLO violations under overload) have been reproduced.

Llama3-8B on Azure Code has been evaluated at varying loads (e.g., 2-6 QPS) under all four schedulers Sarathi-FCFS, Sarathi-SRPF, Sarathi-EDF, and Niyama, with results logged for each QoS bucket (Figure 8).

verification.spec: Run Llama3-8B on Azure Code at loads {2, 3, 4, 5, 6} QPS under Sarathi-FCFS, Sarathi-SRPF, Sarathi-EDF, and Niyama. For each (scheduler, load), record p50 and p95 TTFT for Q1 and p50/p95 TTLT for Q2 and Q3. Verify all six panels of Figure 8 are reproduced and that Niyama's p95 stays below the SLO line at loads where baselines exceed it.

sub_tasks.requirements: Niyama sustains roughly 40% higher load than the best baseline while still meeting tail (p95) latency SLOs across all three QoS buckets.

verification.spec: Sweep QPS for Niyama and the best baseline on Azure-Code (Llama3-8B, A100). For each, find the max QPS where every QoS bucket meets p95 SLO. Compute (Niyama_max / Baseline_max) - 1. Expect ~0.4.

sub_tasks.requirements: Average TBT-violation rate across all schedulers in the overload sweep is below 0.1%, demonstrated by the chosen chunk sizes.

verification.spec: Across the QPS sweep on Azure-Code (Llama3-8B) for all four schedulers, compute the fraction of inter-token intervals that exceed the per-request TBT deadline averaged over schedulers. Expect <0.1%.

sub_tasks.requirements: Niyama maintains zero overall deadline violations up to roughly 30% higher load than the next-best baseline (Sarathi-EDF) (Figure 9a).

verification.spec: Sweep QPS for Niyama and Sarathi-EDF on Azure-Code (Llama3-8B, A100). Identify the max QPS where overall deadline-violation is exactly 0 for each. Compute (Niyama_zero / EDF_zero) - 1. Expect ~0.30.

sub_tasks.requirements: Per-bucket deadline violation curves vs load have been reported for Q1 (TTFT=6s, TBT=50ms), Q2 (TTLT=600s), and Q3 (TTLT=1800s) for all four schedulers (Figure 9d-f).

verification.spec: From the Azure Code Llama3-8B sweep, extract per-QoS-bucket violation percentages vs load for Q1 (TTFT=6s, TBT=50ms), Q2 (TTLT=600s), and Q3 (TTLT=1800s) under Sarathi-FCFS, Sarathi-SRPF, Sarathi-EDF, and Niyama. Verify the three panels of Figure 9d-f are produced and Niyama's curves stay below the baselines'.

sub_tasks.requirements: Sarathi-FCFS is shown to violate the strictest QoS bucket first as load grows due to head-of-line blocking.

verification.spec: From the Figure 9d-f outputs, check that as load increases from 2 to 6 QPS under Sarathi-FCFS, the strictest QoS bucket Q1 begins violating SLOs first and at a higher rate than Q2 and Q3 at each load point.

sub_tasks.requirements: Sarathi-EDF is shown to spread violations roughly equally across all three QoS tiers.

verification.spec: From the Figure 9d-f outputs under Sarathi-EDF, verify that at overload (e.g., 5-6 QPS) the violation percentages across Q1, Q2, and Q3 are roughly equal (within ~15 percentage points of each other), demonstrating EDF spreads violations across tiers.

sub_tasks.requirements: Sarathi-SRPF is shown to maintain low median latency but produce unbounded p95 latency and to drop most long requests even at low loads (~2.5 QPS) per Figure 2.

verification.spec: From the Sarathi-SRPF sweep on Azure Code (Figure 8 / Figure 2), verify (a) p50 TTFT for Q1 remains below the SLO across the load range, (b) p95 TTFT grows much faster than other schedulers as load increases, and (c) at load ~2.5 QPS, more than 50% of long requests (prompts >= p90) violate their SLO.

sub_tasks.requirements: Niyama is shown to achieve fewer long-job deadline violations than Sarathi-SRPF and fewer overall deadline violations than all baselines (Figure 9b, 9c).

verification.spec: From Figure 9 outputs, verify that at every load point in {2,3,4,5,6} QPS, Niyama's long-job violation percentage is lower than Sarathi-SRPF's, and Niyama's overall violation percentage is lower than each of Sarathi-FCFS, Sarathi-SRPF, and Sarathi-EDF.

sub_tasks.requirements: Experiments and numerical results for Section 4.3 (transient overload / diurnal load) have been reproduced.
Code has been implemented to generate a 4-hour diurnal workload that alternates between low load (~2 QPS) and high load (~6 QPS) every 15 minutes (Figure 10a).

verification.spec: Invoke the diurnal workload generator with parameters (low_qps=2, high_qps=6, period_minutes=15, total_hours=4). Verify the generated arrival times form a Poisson stream whose instantaneous rate alternates between ~2 QPS and ~6 QPS every 15 minutes for 4 hours, matching Figure 10a's pattern.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to randomly tag 20% of requests in each QoS bucket as low-priority and the remaining 80% as Important.

verification.spec: Invoke the priority-tagging utility on a synthetic workload of 10,000 requests evenly split across three QoS buckets. Verify that within each bucket approximately 20% (+/- 2%) of requests are marked low_priority and 80% Important, and that tagging is independent across buckets.

sub_tasks.requirements: The diurnal-load experiment has been executed for Sarathi-FCFS, Sarathi-EDF, and Niyama.

verification.spec: Run the 4-hour diurnal-load experiment (Section 4.3) end-to-end for Sarathi-FCFS, Sarathi-EDF, and Niyama using Llama3-8B on an A100. For each scheme, output the overall violation percentage, the Important-request violation percentage, and per-bucket violation percentages, matching the table in Figure 10b within +/- 5 percentage points (Sarathi-FCFS overall ~81.88%, Sarathi-EDF ~84.12%, Niyama ~8.64% with 0% Important violations).

sub_tasks.requirements: Sarathi-FCFS reports an overall violation rate of approximately 81.88% and Important-request violation of approximately 81.96% in the diurnal experiment.

verification.spec: Run the diurnal workload (Figure 10a, QPS oscillating 2->6 every 15 min for 4h, Azure-Code, Llama3-8B, A100) under Sarathi-FCFS. Report overall deadline violation% across all requests; expect ~81.88%.

sub_tasks.requirements: Sarathi-EDF reports an overall violation rate of approximately 84.12% and Important-request violation of approximately 84.09% in the diurnal experiment.

verification.spec: Run the diurnal workload under Sarathi-EDF on Azure-Code/Llama3-8B/A100. Report overall and Important-request violation%; expect ~84.12% and ~84.09% respectively.

sub_tasks.requirements: Niyama reports an overall violation rate of approximately 8.64% and an Important-request violation rate of 0% in the diurnal experiment.

verification.spec: Run the diurnal workload under Niyama on Azure-Code/Llama3-8B/A100 with 20% requests tagged low-priority. Report overall violation% (~8.64) and Important violation% (~0).

sub_tasks.requirements: Per-bucket violation rates of Niyama for QoS 0 (~16.03%), QoS 1 (~9.98%), and QoS 2 (~0%) in the diurnal experiment match Figure 10(b).

verification.spec: From the same Niyama diurnal run, break down violations by QoS tier. Expect QoS0~16.03%, QoS1~9.98%, QoS2~0%.

sub_tasks.requirements: Niyama meets p99 latency SLOs for all Important requests and for at least 92% of all requests in the diurnal experiment (Figure 11).

verification.spec: From the diurnal Niyama run, compute the fraction of all requests meeting their p99 latency SLO. Expect >=0.92. Also verify all Important requests pass.

sub_tasks.requirements: Ablation studies (Section 4.4) have been reproduced.
Code has been implemented to ablate Niyama by toggling Dynamic Chunking (DC), Eager Relegation (ER), and Hybrid Prioritization (HP) on top of the Sarathi-EDF baseline.

verification.spec: Verify the experiment runner exposes boolean flags (or configs) to independently enable Dynamic Chunking, Eager Relegation, and Hybrid Prioritization on top of the Sarathi-EDF baseline. Run with configurations {EDF, EDF+DC, EDF+DC+ER, EDF+DC+ER+HP} on a small synthetic workload and confirm the runner accepts each combination and produces a distinct output log indicating which features are active.

sub_tasks.requirements: Adding only Dynamic Chunking (Niyama-DC) increases the optimal-load QPS from 2.75 (Sarathi-EDF) to roughly 3.3, a ~20% improvement.

verification.spec: Run Sarathi-EDF and Niyama-with-DC-only (no ER, no HP) on Azure-Code/Llama3-8B/A100. Sweep QPS to find max sustained load with <=1% violations. Expect EDF~2.75 QPS and Niyama-DC~3.30 QPS (~20% improvement).

sub_tasks.requirements: Adding Eager Relegation on top of DC (Niyama-DC+ER) raises the optimal-load QPS to roughly 3.6, a ~9% incremental gain.

verification.spec: Add Eager Relegation on top of DC and sweep QPS on Azure-Code/Llama3-8B/A100 to find max sustained load with <=1% violations. Expect ~3.6 QPS.

sub_tasks.requirements: Adding Hybrid Prioritization on top of DC+ER (full Niyama) reaches an optimal-load QPS of approximately 3.65 (~1.4% incremental gain).

verification.spec: Enable DC+ER+HP (full Niyama) and sweep QPS on Azure-Code/Llama3-8B/A100 to find max sustained load with <=1% violations. Expect ~3.65 QPS.

sub_tasks.requirements: At high load (QPS=6), the high-load violation percentage decreases from 100% (Sarathi-EDF) to 74% (DC), 26% (DC+ER), and 16% (DC+ER+HP).

verification.spec: At QPS=6 on Azure-Code/Llama3-8B/A100, run Sarathi-EDF, Niyama-DC, Niyama-DC+ER, and full Niyama; report % deadline violations. Expect 100, 74, 26, 16 respectively.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to sweep the hybrid prioritization parameter alpha over the values 0, 2, and 4 and to plot median latency and overall deadline violation as a function of load (Figure 12).

verification.spec: Run the alpha sweep with alpha in {0, 2, 4} across loads {2,3,4,5,6} QPS on Llama3-8B/Azure-Code. Output median latency vs load and overall deadline violation % vs load for each alpha. Verify two plots matching Figure 12 are produced.

sub_tasks.requirements: The alpha sweep demonstrates that higher alpha reduces median latency but increases long-job deadline violations, as illustrated by Figure 12.

verification.spec: From the alpha sweep results, verify that at fixed load (e.g., 5 QPS) median latency monotonically decreases as alpha grows from 0 to 4, while long-job violation percentage monotonically increases as alpha grows from 0 to 4.

sub_tasks.requirements: An ablation has been performed to compare 'no relegation' vs 'eager relegation' showing that relegating ~5% of requests keeps median latency near the SLO, while no relegation lets median latency grow approximately exponentially as load grows (Figure 5).

verification.spec: Run the relegation ablation at loads {3.0, 3.5, 4.0} QPS on Llama3-8B/Azure-Code with eager_relegation toggled on and off. Record median latency and the fraction of relegated requests for each (load, config). Verify that with eager relegation, ~5% of requests are relegated and median latency stays near the SLO, while with no relegation, median latency grows by >=10x from 3.0 to 4.0 QPS, matching Figure 5.

sub_tasks.requirements: Motivational analyses (Section 2.4 / Figure 2 / Figure 4) have been reproduced.

Code has been implemented to reproduce the throughput-vs-latency curve as a function of chunk size, showing that chunk size 330 satisfies a 50ms TBT SLO and that increasing chunk size yields up to ~28% throughput improvement (Figure 4).

verification.spec: On Llama3-8B/A100, profile prefill iteration latency and throughput across chunk sizes from 128 to 2048. Verify (a) chunk size ~330 yields per-iteration latency <=50ms, (b) the throughput gap between chunk=330 and large chunk (~2K) is approximately 28% (i.e. (T_large - T_330)/T_large ~ 0.28).

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to sweep load (1-6 QPS) and compare median TTFT, p99 TTFT, overall violation %, and long-job violation % across FCFS, SRPF, SJF, EDF, and Niyama (Figure 2).

verification.spec: Run the multi-scheduler load sweep across loads {1,2,3,4,5,6} QPS for FCFS, SRPF, SJF, EDF, and Niyama on Llama3-8B / Azure-Code, restricted to the strictest QoS bucket. Output four plots (median TTFT, p99 TTFT, overall violation %, long-job violation %) vs load, matching the four panels of Figure 2.

sub_tasks.requirements: FCFS shows rapid degradation (high median and tail TTFT and high violation rates) as load increases in the reproduced sweep.

verification.spec: From the Figure 2 sweep, verify FCFS curves: median TTFT and p99 TTFT grow by >=10x as load increases from 2 to 6 QPS, and overall violation % exceeds 50% by load 4 QPS.

sub_tasks.requirements: EDF achieves near-zero violations at low load but saturates to high violation rates beyond the capacity threshold in the reproduced sweep.

verification.spec: From the Figure 2 sweep, verify EDF violation % is <=1% at load 2-3 QPS and saturates to >=80% at load 5-6 QPS, demonstrating the abrupt capacity threshold described in the paper.

sub_tasks.requirements: SJF/SRPF preserve median TTFT but produce SLO violations for the majority of long requests even at low loads (~2.5 QPS) in the reproduced sweep.

verification.spec: From the Figure 2 sweep, verify that under SJF and SRPF, the long-job deadline violation percentage exceeds 50% at load 2.5 QPS, while their median TTFT remains close to the SLO across the load range.

sub_tasks.requirements: Additional coverage added by critique pass
Numerical claims from paper tables/figures

Code has been implemented and run such that Niyama achieves a normalized GPU usage of 0.68 (i.e., 32% fewer GPUs) on the Azure Code dataset compared to SOTA Sarathi-Serve siloed deployment baseline (normalized to 1.0) when serving a fixed traffic load of 50 QPS split equally across 3 QoS tiers.

verification.spec: Run capacity benchmark on Azure Code dataset with Llama3-8B on A100; compute number of GPUs needed for SOTA siloed baseline and Niyama to sustain 50 QPS with <=1% SLO violations; assert |measured_ratio - 0.68| / 0.68 <= 0.20

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that Niyama achieves a normalized GPU usage of 0.88 (i.e., 12% fewer GPUs) on the Azure Conv dataset compared to SOTA Sarathi-Serve siloed deployment baseline (normalized to 1.0) when serving a fixed traffic load of 50 QPS split equally across 3 QoS tiers.

verification.spec: Run capacity benchmark on Azure Conv dataset with Llama3-8B on A100; compute GPU count ratio for Niyama vs SOTA Sarathi siloed baseline to sustain 50 QPS at <=1% SLO violation; assert |measured_ratio - 0.88| / 0.88 <= 0.20

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that Niyama's eager relegation mechanism relegates 0% of requests at QPS=3.0 load.

verification.spec: Run Niyama with eager relegation enabled at QPS=3.0 on the configured workload; record fraction of requests sent to relegated queue; assert |relegation_rate - 0.0| <= 0.02 (absolute tolerance since paper value is 0)

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that Niyama's eager relegation mechanism relegates approximately 2% of requests at QPS=3.5 load.

verification.spec: Run Niyama with eager relegation enabled at QPS=3.5; measure fraction of requests relegated; assert |measured - 0.02| / 0.02 <= 0.50 (loose tolerance due to small value)

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that Niyama's eager relegation mechanism relegates approximately 5% of requests at QPS=4.0 load.

verification.spec: Run Niyama with eager relegation enabled at QPS=4.0; measure fraction of requests relegated; assert |measured - 0.05| / 0.05 <= 0.30

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that at QPS=4.0, Niyama with eager relegation enabled maintains median latency near the SLO target, while Niyama without relegation exhibits median latency at least 10x higher (order-of-magnitude growth due to cascading violations).

verification.spec: Run Niyama at QPS=4.0 with and without eager relegation; measure median TTFT in each case; assert ratio (no_relegation_median / eager_relegation_median) >= 10.0 within tolerance 0.30

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that under 50% overload (load 50% above capacity), Niyama maintains latency SLOs for at least 95% of requests.

verification.spec: Determine Niyama's optimal capacity QPS; run Niyama at 1.5x that QPS; measure fraction of requests meeting their per-tier SLOs; assert measured >= 0.95 within tolerance 0.05 absolute (i.e. >=0.90)

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that using a small chunk size required to meet interactive latency SLOs results in approximately 28% lower throughput compared to using a large chunk size optimized for throughput (Figure 4 throughput-vs-chunk-size characterization).

verification.spec: Profile Llama3-8B on A100 with chunk sizes from 256 to 2048; measure throughput at chunk=330 (interactive-feasible, 50ms TBT SLO) and at the throughput-maximizing chunk size; assert |1 - (low_chunk_thr / high_chunk_thr) - 0.28| / 0.28 <= 0.25

sub_tasks.paper_unit: % throughput reduction from using small chunk size

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that Niyama increases overall serving capacity by approximately 32% compared to SOTA Sarathi-Serve siloed deployment while maintaining QoS guarantees (the headline result across datasets).

verification.spec: Compare max sustainable QPS (at <=1% SLO violation) for Niyama vs Sarathi-Serve siloed deployment on Azure Code workload mix across 3 QoS tiers using Llama3-8B/A100; assert (Niyama_capacity / SOTA_capacity - 1) is within 0.20 relative tolerance of 0.32

sub_tasks.paper_unit: % increase in serving capacity vs SOTA siloed

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that under extreme load (transient bursty workload of the §4.3 scenario), Niyama reduces SLO violations by at least an order of magnitude (~10x) compared to SOTA Sarathi-FCFS and Sarathi-EDF strategies.

verification.spec: Run diurnal workload (varying QPS 2-6 over 4h, see Figure 10) for Niyama, Sarathi-FCFS, Sarathi-EDF on Llama3-8B/A100; measure overall %SLO violations; assert (baseline_violation_pct / Niyama_violation_pct) >= 10.0 within tolerance 0.30

sub_tasks.paper_unit: x reduction in SLO violations vs SOTA under extreme load

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that under the dynamic diurnal workload of §4.3, Niyama's overall SLO violation rate is approximately 8.64% (paper-reported value), compared to ~81-84% for Sarathi-FCFS and Sarathi-EDF baselines.

verification.spec: Run the dynamic workload of Figure 10(a) (alternating QPS=2 low and QPS=6 high every 15min for 4h, 20% low-priority requests) for Niyama with full optimizations on Llama3-8B/A100; assert |measured_violation_pct - 8.64| / 8.64 <= 0.30

sub_tasks.paper_unit: % overall SLO violations under diurnal overload

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that under the dynamic diurnal workload of §4.3, Niyama achieves 0% SLO violations for high-priority (Important) requests (where 80% of requests in each QoS bucket are tagged important based on application hints).

verification.spec: Run dynamic workload of Figure 10 with 20% low-priority + 80% high-priority labelling; measure fraction of high-priority requests violating their per-tier SLO under Niyama; assert measured <= 0.02 (absolute tolerance since paper value is 0)

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that under transient overload conditions at 50% above system capacity, Niyama meets latency SLO targets for at least 95% of requests across all QoS tiers.

verification.spec: Run the Niyama scheduler on the Azure-Code workload at 1.5x the measured serving capacity using the QoS bucket configuration from Table 2; record per-request SLO compliance (TTFT/TBT for interactive, TTLT for non-interactive); compute fraction of requests meeting SLO; assert measured >= 0.95 within tolerance

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the latency/violation curves of Figure 8 are reproduced showing FCFS/SRPF/EDF/Niyama across loads on the Azure-Code dataset with Llama3-8B.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that in the 4-hour transient-overload (diurnal) experiment, Niyama misses deadlines for 0 important requests and ~8.75% of all requests, while baselines (Sarathi-FCFS, Sarathi-EDF) violate >80% overall.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the combined gain from Dynamic Chunking + Eager Relegation over Sarathi-EDF baseline yields a 68% reduction in SLO violations at high load (QPS=6) on Azure-Code with Llama3-8B on a single A100

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduce paper's reported baselines
Code has been implemented and run such that Sarathi-EDF goodput on Azure Conv dataset is approximately 2.75 QPS per replica when scheduling across all QoS tiers on a shared A100 cluster.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that Sarathi-FCFS goodput on Azure Conv dataset is approximately 2.75 QPS per replica when scheduling across all QoS tiers on a shared A100 cluster (matching Sarathi-EDF).

sub_tasks.requirements: Ablation studies from the paper

Code has been implemented and run such that at high load (QPS=6), incremental ablations over Sarathi-EDF show the following relative reductions in % SLO violations: Dynamic Chunking alone reduces violations by ~26% (from 100% to 74%), adding Eager Relegation reduces by ~68% (from 74% to 26%), and additionally adding Hybrid Prioritization reduces by ~32% (from 26% to 16%).

verification.spec: Run four configurations (Sarathi-EDF, +DC, +DC+ER, +DC+ER+HP) at QPS=6 on Azure Code with Llama3-8B/A100; measure %SLO violations for each; compute the three pairwise relative reductions; assert each is within 20% relative tolerance of paper values [26, 68, 32]

sub_tasks.paper_unit: % relative reduction in violations from adding Eager Relegation

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that adding dynamic chunking to Sarathi-EDF improves high-load violation rate by 26% (ablation Table 3 row Niyama-DC).

verification.spec: Run ablation(qps=6, schemes=['sarathi_edf','niyama_dc_only']); compute (viol_edf - viol_dc) / viol_edf; assert relative improvement within tolerance of 0.26

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that adding dynamic chunking to Sarathi-EDF improves the optimal-load throughput goodput by 9% (Table 3 ablation Niyama-DC QPS gain).

verification.spec: Run ablation(scheme='niyama_dc_er'); compute optimal QPS gain over preceding row; assert within tolerance of 9%

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that an ablation comparing selective preemption (preempt prefill only) vs no preemption vs preempt-decodes-allowed is reported, showing prefill-only preemption strictly dominates on overall SLO violation rate

verification.spec: Run three Niyama variants (no preemption, prefill-only preemption, preempt decodes too) on Azure-Code with Llama3-8B at QPS=4; record overall %SLO violations; assert prefill-only achieves lowest violations and that decode-preemption variant shows >2x higher TBT violation rate than prefill-only

sub_tasks.paper_unit: x ratio of TBT violations (decode-preempt / prefill-only)

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that an ablation of non-interactive decode-length estimation compares the paper's 2-sigma over-approximation vs alternatives (mean, p99, p50), with 2-sigma yielding the lowest TTLT SLO violation rate

verification.spec: Run Niyama with each estimator (mean, p50, p99, mean+2*std) on Azure-Conv with Llama3-8B at moderate-high load; measure TTLT deadline violation %; assert mean+2sigma yields lowest or within 10% of lowest

sub_tasks.paper_unit: relative violation ratio (2-sigma vs best alternative)

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that an ablation comparing fixed chunk size 256 vs dynamic chunking under the strictest TBT class (50ms) demonstrates dynamic chunking gives >=15% goodput improvement while maintaining <1% TBT violations

verification.spec: Run Niyama with fixed chunk=256 and with dynamic chunking on Azure-Code with Llama3-8B; find max QPS sustained at <=1% SLO violation; compute (qps_dynamic - qps_fixed)/qps_fixed; assert >=0.15 and TBT violations <1% in both
verification.paper_cite: Section 4.4, Table 3 DC row (+20% over Sarathi-EDF baseline)

sub_tasks.requirements: Hardware / library platforms used in the paper
Code has been implemented and run such that the evaluation workload is partitioned into three QoS buckets (Q1, Q2, Q3) with equal 33.33% request ratio each, as specified in Table 2.

verification.spec: Run the workload generator that assigns QoS classes; count fraction of requests in each of Q1/Q2/Q3 buckets; assert each fraction is within tolerance of 0.3333

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that in the transient-overload diurnal workload, 20% of requests in each QoS bucket are randomly tagged as low-priority based on application hints.

verification.spec: Run the diurnal workload generator; count fraction of low-priority requests per QoS bucket; assert each fraction is within tolerance of 0.20

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the remaining 80% of requests per QoS bucket are tagged as high-priority/Important in the transient-overload evaluation.

verification.spec: Run diurnal workload generator; assert fraction of important requests per bucket within tolerance of 0.80

sub_tasks.requirements: Environment is configured with NVIDIA A100 80GB GPU available and torch.cuda detects an A100 device with >=80GB memory

verification.spec: import torch; assert torch.cuda.is_available(); name = torch.cuda.get_device_name(0); assert 'A100' in name; assert torch.cuda.get_device_properties(0).total_memory >= 79*1024**3

sub_tasks.requirements: Environment is configured to run Qwen-7B with tensor parallelism TP=2 across two A100 GPUs and a single forward pass completes successfully

verification.spec: Launch vLLM/Sarathi-Serve with model=Qwen/Qwen-7B and tensor_parallel_size=2; submit a short prompt; assert non-empty completion returned and both GPUs show >0 utilization during the request

sub_tasks.requirements: vLLM inference engine is installed and serves as the base framework; importing vllm and instantiating an LLM engine succeeds

verification.spec: import vllm; from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams; assert callable(LLM); assert hasattr(vllm, '__version__')

sub_tasks.requirements: The Sarathi-Serve scheduler codebase is available and Niyama extends its scheduler class; importing the Sarathi base scheduler succeeds and Niyama scheduler subclasses it

verification.spec: Import sarathi base scheduler module and niyama scheduler module; assert NiyamaScheduler is a subclass of (or wraps) the SarathiScheduler class

sub_tasks.requirements: The Vidur LLM inference simulator is integrated and used to generate (chunk_size, batch_state) -> latency profiles; a profile dataset file exists with >=1000 entries

verification.spec: Locate the Vidur-generated profile dataset (e.g., csv/parquet); assert file exists, columns include chunk_size and latency, and row count >= 1000

sub_tasks.requirements: Method components (from critique splits and additions)
Code has been implemented such that the TTFT deadline equation for interactive requests (rendered as figure of width ~177px) is implemented per the paper's definition.

verification.spec: Import the TTFT deadline computation function; for a sample request with arrival_time=t0 and TTFT SLO=s, assert deadline_ttft(req) == t0 + s

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented such that the per-token deadline equation for subsequent decode tokens (TBT-based, rendered as figure of width ~209px) is implemented per the paper's definition.

verification.spec: Import the per-token deadline function; for a request with TTFT deadline d_ttft and TBT SLO b, assert deadline_token(req, n) == d_ttft + (n-1)*b for n>=2

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented such that the TTLT deadline equation for non-interactive requests (rendered as figure of width ~175px) is implemented per the paper's definition.

verification.spec: Import the TTLT deadline function; for a non-interactive request with arrival_time=t0 and TTLT SLO=s, assert deadline_ttlt(req) == t0 + s

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented such that the hybrid prioritization equation for interactive requests (priority = alpha*TTFT_deadline + (1-alpha)*Prefill_rem, rendered ~203px wide) is implemented as a linear combination per Section 3.4.

verification.spec: Import hybrid_priority_interactive(req, alpha); construct two requests with known TTFT deadlines and prefill_remaining; verify priority is a linear interpolation: priority(alpha=0) == prefill_rem ordering (SRPF), priority(alpha=1) == TTFT-deadline ordering (EDF)

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented such that the hybrid prioritization equation for non-interactive requests (using TTLT deadline and Prefill_rem + Decode_rem, rendered ~237px wide) is implemented per Section 3.4.

verification.spec: Import hybrid_priority_noninteractive(req, alpha); given known TTLT deadline, prefill_rem, decode_rem (estimated from history+2*std), verify priority equals the linear interpolation of EDF on TTLT and SRPF on (prefill_rem + decode_rem)

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the serving throughput per replica is defined as the maximum sustained load with at most 1% deadline violations, and this definition is enforced in the capacity-computation code.

verification.spec: Run the capacity-finding routine; verify the binary-search/sweep accepts a load only when violation rate <= 1%; assert the threshold parameter equals 1%

sub_tasks.requirements: A latency-predictor module is implemented exposing a function predict_iteration_latency(chunk_size, decode_batch_state) that returns predicted iteration latency in milliseconds

verification.spec: Import predictor module; call predict_iteration_latency(chunk_size=256, decode_batch_state={'num_decodes':4,'avg_context_len':1024}); assert returns a positive float in plausible range (1-1000 ms)
verification.paper_cite: Section 3.6 Implementation, dynamic chunk size predictor

sub_tasks.requirements: A random-forest regression model is trained on Vidur-generated (config, latency) pairs and serialized to disk; the saved model loads and produces predictions

verification.spec: Load the serialized random-forest model (e.g., joblib/pickle); assert it is a RandomForestRegressor instance; predict on a held-out batch and assert R^2 >= 0.85 against Vidur ground truth

sub_tasks.requirements: The Niyama scheduler loads the trained latency predictor at initialization and invokes it on every scheduling iteration to bound the chosen chunk size

verification.spec: Monkey-patch the predictor to log calls; instantiate NiyamaScheduler and run 50 scheduling iterations on synthetic requests; assert predictor was called >=50 times and that the chosen chunk size in each iter satisfies predicted_latency <= slack-based bound

sub_tasks.requirements: A function compute_min_slack(decode_requests, now) is implemented that returns the minimum slack (next-token deadline minus current time) across all in-flight decode requests for the current iteration

verification.spec: Construct 3 mock decode requests with known next-token deadlines [t+10ms, t+50ms, t+100ms]; call compute_min_slack(reqs, t); assert returned value == 10ms (within 0.5ms)

sub_tasks.requirements: A function choose_chunk_size(slack, decode_state, max_chunk) is implemented that performs a search (e.g., binary search) over candidate chunk sizes using the latency predictor and returns the largest chunk whose predicted latency <= slack

verification.spec: Stub predictor as latency = chunk_size * 0.1 ms; call choose_chunk_size(slack=50ms, decode_state=..., max_chunk=2048); assert returned chunk in [490, 510] (the largest chunk with predicted latency <=50ms)

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented such that a dynamic chunking function returns the largest chunk size whose predicted iteration latency is at most the minimum decode-slack across in-flight decode requests, and is clipped to a per-QoS-class TBT-derived upper bound (e.g., chunk_size=256 for the 50ms TBT class).

verification.spec: Import the dynamic chunking selector. Construct synthetic decode-queue states with varying per-request slack values and a mock latency predictor with known monotonic latency-vs-chunk-size curve. Assert: (a) returned chunk size is the largest value whose predicted latency <= min(slack); (b) when an interactive request with strict TBT is present, the returned chunk size does not exceed the configured per-class cap (e.g., 256 for 50ms TBT); (c) when only relaxed (non-interactive) requests are in the decode queue, the function selects a larger chunk size (e.g., >= 1024) given sufficient slack.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented such that a bursty overload workload matching the Figure 1 (bottom-left) QPS profile is generated, with arrival times alternating between low- and high-QPS phases over a ~15000s horizon and requests drawn from one of the evaluated datasets (e.g., Azure Code or Azure Conv).

verification.spec: Invoke the trace-generation function for the bursty scenario. Assert: (a) total trace duration is ~15000s (within 10%); (b) the QPS time series contains at least two distinct levels with a high-phase QPS at least 2x the low-phase QPS; (c) inter-arrival times within a phase are consistent with a Poisson process (e.g., chi-square or KS test on exponential inter-arrivals, p>0.01); (d) each request carries a QoS-class label and prompt/decode token counts sampled from the dataset distribution.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that both the Sarathi-Serve baseline and Niyama are executed end-to-end on the bursty overload trace, producing per-request TTFT/TBT/TTLT logs for each system.

verification.spec: Run the experiment driver that schedules the bursty trace through (a) Sarathi-Serve with FCFS and (b) Niyama (dynamic chunking + hybrid prioritization + eager relegation). Assert both runs complete without error and write per-request result logs containing arrival_time, completion_time, ttft, qos_class, deadline, and a violation flag. Assert log row count equals the number of requests in the trace for each system.
